front: Artist: best known for black-and-white landscape photographs of this California national park's Half Dome, El Capitan, and other formations
Photograph: shows the moon rising over a cemetery in a New Mexico town; Adams pulled off Route 84 to capture it

Photograph: early Adams image of the granite formation Half Dome in Yosemite, taken from the 'Diving Board' using a deep-red filter to create a jet-black sky; published in The Parmelian Prints of the High Sierras
back: Monolith, the Face of Half Dome (Ansel Adams)
work: Monolith, the Face of Half Dome

front: Artist: co-developed the Zone System with Fred Archer, rating levels of photographic exposure and brightness from zero to ten
Artist: co-founded Group f/64 with Edward Weston, Imogen Cunningham, and Willard Van Dyke; the group's name refers to an aperture setting on large-format cameras
Work: photo collection documenting Japanese-American internment at Manzanar, California; Adams was invited by camp director Ralph Merritt
Work: Adams's first published book, co-written with poet Mary Austin, documenting the culture of a New Mexico pueblo; Adams met Paul Strand during its creation
Artist: Alfred Stieglitz hosted one of this American landscape photographer's first major exhibitions in 1936
Artist: was inspired by Paul Strand to abandon Pictorialism and move toward straight photography
Artist: photographed The Tetons and Snake River at Grand Teton National Park; also photographed White House Ruin in Canyon de Chelly National Monument, Arizona
Artist: his baseball photographs from Manzanar inspired Mike Mandel to invite Adams and 133 other photographers to pose as baseball players
